coredata.dc:description: The ongoing poisoning of agricultural products has pushed the security problem to become an important issue. Among them, exceeding the standard rate of pesticide residues is the main factor influencing the quality and security of agricultural products. Moreover, the abuse of pesticides has introduced a large amount of residues in soil and drinking water, which will enter the food chain to the human body, leading to neurological disorders and cancer. Therefore, great efforts have been devoted to developing fluorescent sensors for detecting pesticide in a facile, quickly, sensitive, selective, accurate manner, which exhibit greater advantages than some traditional methods. In this review, we mainly focus on summarizing the non-biological fluorescent probes for organic pesticides detection with the detection limit of micromole to nanomole, including organic functional small molecules, calixarenes and pillararenes, metal organic framework systems, and nanomaterials. Meanwhile, we described the different sensing mechanisms for pesticides detection of these mentioned fluorescent sensors, the detection limit of each pesticide, the application in detecting actual samples, as well as their respective advantages and development prospects associated with present non-biological fluorescent sensors.

head.ce:title: Non-biological fluorescent chemosensors for pesticides detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ongoing poisoning of agricultural products has pushed the security problem to become an important issue. Among them, exceeding the standard rate of pesticide residues is the main factor influencing the quality and security of agricultural products. Moreover, the abuse of pesticides has introduced a large amount of residues in soil and drinking water, which will enter the food chain to the human body, leading to neurological disorders and cancer. Therefore, great efforts have been devoted to developing fluorescent sensors for detecting pesticide in a facile, quickly, sensitive, selective, accurate manner, which exhibit greater advantages than some traditional methods. In this review, we mainly focus on summarizing the non-biological fluorescent probes for organic pesticides detection with the detection limit of micromole to nanomole, including organic functional small molecules, calixarenes and pillararenes, metal organic framework systems, and nanomaterials. Meanwhile, we described the different sensing mechanisms for pesticides detection of these mentioned fluorescent sensors, the detection limit of each pesticide, the application in detecting actual samples, as well as their respective advantages and development prospects associated with present non-biological fluorescent sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: In the rapid and efficient development of agriculture, pesticides play a vital role in the growth protection for crops, and the storage and transportation of agricultural products [1,2]. The use of pesticides can reduce the number of pests and weeds to increase the yield of agricultural products [3,4]. However, even trace levels to pesticide residues can seriously cause food pollution, severely damage the ecological environment [5,6], and pose a great threat to people's lives [7,8]. Due to the aquatic usage pattern and high environmental stability of most pesticides, abuse of pesticides has introduced a large number of residues in soil and drinking water, which will enter the food chain to the human body, ultimately. As a result, pesticide pollution has attracted more and more attention as one of the most worrying challenges [9,10]. Therefore, the sensitive detection and analysis of pesticide residues is an urgent need to ensure food quality and human safety [11,12].

Currently, the most common methods of detecting pesticides include gas chromatography (GC) [13,14], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [15,16], gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [17,18]. Although these methods have high selectivity, adequate sensitivity, and allow discrimination of pesticides among complex samples, these instrumental analysis methods have the distinct disadvantages of complicated sampling techniques, cumbersome processes, and the need for professional technicians [19]. In consequence, there is an urgent need for simple, rapid, and easy-to-use methods for the sensitive and selective detection of pesticide residues in the environment.

Fluorescent detection is one of the most convenient methods of various detection methods of low detection limit, high sensitivity, simple operation, fast response speed, and the visible detection mode by the naked eye [20,21]. Over the past decade, significant efforts have been devoted to developing different kinds of fluorescent chemosensors to selectively detect pesticide residues [22,23]. Several review papers have been published on fluorescent sensors for pesticides detection. For example, in 2015, Kumar et al. have reviewed the sensors based on functional materials for detecting organophosphorus pesticides, including fluorescent nanomaterial-based sensors, molecularly imprinted sensors, electrochemical sensors, and biosensors [24]. In 2018, Yan et al. have reviewed optical sensors for pesticides detection with enzymes, antibodies, molecularly-imprinted polymers, aptamers, and host-guest reactions as recognition elements [25]. Pundir et al. have described fluorescent sensors with enzymes as recognition elements for detecting organophosphorus pesticides in 2019 [26]. These reviews mainly focused on biological reaction-based optical detection for pesticides. In this review, we mainly summarize the development of non-biological fluorescent sensors for pesticides detection in the past 8 years, as shown in Table S1. According to the materials (hosts) used to construct the non-biological fluorescent sensors, non-biological fluorescent sensors are divided into the following four categories: small-molecule based chemosensors, host-gest based hydrocarbon chemosensors, metal organic frameworks (MOFs), and nanomaterial based chemosensors. Meanwhile, the detection mechanism of each type of non-biological fluorescent chemosensor for corresponding pesticides will be discussed, as well as the advantage, the detection limit, and the actual application of these respective chemosensors. Besides discussing the points mentioned above, we also present the existing deficiencies and current challenges, as well as the future perspectives of present chemosensors on in situ detection.
Small-molecule based chemosensors have the advantage of response-rapidity and high.

specificity in detection [27,28]. Pesticides usually can be accurately recognized by small-molecule chemosensors including common organic compounds and metal complexes through specific reactions such as click reaction, electrostatic interaction, complexation reaction, or specific binding site. For example, Lu et al. have reported a weakly fluorescent compound 3-azido-7-hydroxycoumarin as a turn-on sensor to detect flumioxazin [29]. Based on click chemistry, the probe reacting with flumioxazin produced strong fluorescent at 475 nm in the PBS buffer solution (0.01 M, pH = 7.0) through the Cu+-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition reaction (Fig. 1 a). Using this method, the limit of detection could reach 0.18 μg/L, which was about 50 times lower than that of GC and HPLC. This fluorescent sensor exhibited not only a low limit of detection, but also high selectivity due to the specific reaction to azide and alkyne. It has been applied to the detection of residual flumioxazin in some vegetables and water samples.

Also, a turn-on fluorescent sensor for paraquat detection using an assembly of squaraine (SQ) and surfactants has been reported by Tu et al. [30]. When the electron-deficient paraquat was combined with the anionic surfactant sodium lauryl sulfate on the surface of the SQ dyes, SQ dyes changed from aggregate state to dispersed state, resulting in strong fluorescent at 660 nm (Fig. 1b). This method could selectively identify paraquat with a detection limit of 95.7 μg/L, which was lower than the value recommended by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (200 μg/L). In addition, this sensor has also been used to detect the paraquat in actual samples such as Chinese cabbage.

Kaur et al. have synthesized two different Biginelli derivatives for fluorescent turn-on detection of organophosphorus pesticides which were engineered via self-assembly of the pyridyl-2-cobalt complex [L1·Co(II)] and the pyridyl-4-cobalt complex [L2·Co(II)] (Scheme S1-compounds 1 and 2) [31]. The fluorescent emission of L1·Co(II) at 426 nm was gradually increased with the addition of malathion. In the presence of azamethiphos, the fluorescent emission of L2·Co(II) at 355 nm enhanced and a redshift was observed on 370 nm, simultaneously. The limit of detection for malathion and azamethiphos was 9.2 nM and 11 nM in the aqueous medium, respectively. The detection mechanism was that the electronegative oxygen and sulfur atom in malathion and azamethiphos could bind to the hydrogen atom of the secondary amine of the pyrimidine ring, as shown in Scheme S1-compound 3 and compound 4, resulting in the formation of a partial negative charge on the nitrogen atom of the pyrimidine ring which delocalized from the conjugated imidazole ring, thereby increasing the interaction with the Co(II) ion. The electron-rich nitrogen atom further promoted the transfer of protons from the pyrimidine ring, resulting in an increase in emission intensity.

In addition to fluorescent turn-on sensors, fluorescent turn-off sensors also made outstanding contributions to pesticide detection. For example, Zhao et al. have reported a simple fluorescent turn-off sensor pyranine for paraquat detection [32]. Since pyranine is a sensitive fluorescent material, it was related to the acid-base balance of hydroxide groups in an aqueous solution. Paraquat had a special ability to selectively interact with pyranine (Fig. 2 a). With the addition of paraquat, the fluorescent of the probe pyranine at 511 nm was quenched. This work explored the quenching of pyranine and paraquat at different pH. Experiments showed that the fluorescent change of pyranine was significantly correlated with paraquat at pH = 7. In addition, the effect of pyranine concentration on the experiment was also explored. Paraquat was most sensitive to pyranine when the pyranine concentration was 4 mM. The detection limit of this method was 0.2 μM and this method can be applied to paraquat detection in tap water and natural water [collected from Dongpu reservoir (Hefei, China)].

Fan et al. have synthesized the host-guest complex using the dyes thioflavin T (ThT) and twisted cucurbit [14] uril (tQ[14]) as a fluorescent turn-off sensor to detect flusilazole [33], which was used to prevent powdery mildew. The ThT@tQ[14] probe displayed a selective response to flusilazole, and the fluorescent quenching at 488 nm was remarkable with the detection limit of 12.7 nM. The freely rotating dimethylamine group on the phenyl group of ThT was restricted to the tQ[14] complex, resulting in the fluorescent of ThT enhanced. At the same time, the fluorescent emission of ThT was also affected by its electron donating group (dimethylamino moiety) and electron withdrawing group (quaternary ammonium moiety). After adding flusilazole, the interaction of the azole moiety in flusilazole with the quaternary ammonium moiety of ThT weakened the electron-withdrawing ability of the quaternary ammonium group, resulting in partial quenching of the fluorescent of tQ[14] (Fig. 2b).

Wu et al. used a visible naked-eye fluorescent turn-off and colorimetric probe to detect fluoride anions and oxyfluorfen. With the addition of fluoride ion or oxyfluorfen [34], the color of the solution changed from colorless to yellow, and the fluorescent emission at 443 nm was quenched in CH3CN. The probe provided a strong hydrogen bond donor to form a strong hydrogen bond interaction with fluoride anion or oxyfluorfen (Fig. 2c). The detection limit of oxyfluorfen was 0.83 μM. In the presence of some small molecules with a similar structure to oxyfluorfen but without –CF3 group, the probe showed excellent sensitivity due to the π-π interaction between the conjugated system of oxyfluorfen. Meanwhile, the probe increased the density of the electron cloud of –CF3 group, promoting the interaction of hydrogen bond between oxyfluorfen and the probe. Compared fluorescent turn-on sensors with fluorescent turn-off sensors, the fluorescent turn-on sensors has better detection sensitivity, because the fluorescent turn-off sensors are easily interfered by other environmental factors, such as the fluorescent turn-off caused by the solvent.

Among common organic compounds, metal organic complexes exhibit excellent performance and can produce a fluorescent response to a variety of pesticides. For example, Azab et al. have synthesized a series of Tb3+-complex and Eu3+-complex to detect pesticides. They firstly synthesized a fluorescent probe in the ratio of Tb3+: ligand (Scheme S2, compound 7) in 1:3 stoichiometric to detect chlorfenvinphos and dichlorvos in methanol [35]. Stern-Volmer studies at different temperatures demonstrated that collisional quenching played a dominant role, namely dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching was caused by the interaction between fluorophore and quencher during the lifetime of the excited state. Fluorescent emission at 545 nm of the probe was quenched with the addition of these two pesticides. Subsequently, they have prepared the polymer using this probe as a monomer and studied the interaction between the polymer and chlorfenvinphos and dichlorvos pesticides with the detection limit of 0.43 and 0.32 μM, respectively.

Subsequently, they have synthesized a Eu3+ complex (Scheme S2, compound 8) as a fluorescent probe and studied its interaction with the pesticides chlorpyrifos, malathion, endosulfan, and heptachlor in PIPES buffer [piperazine-N,N′-bis(2-ethanesulfonic acid)] (pH = 7.5, 0.003 M) [36]. The complex had high stability and great efficient luminescence at pH = 7.5. With the addition of malathion, the fluorescent emission at 613 nm of the complex was quenched. However, with the addition of endosulfan, heptachlor, and chlorpyrifos, the fluorescent emission at 613 nm of the complex was enhanced. Experiments showed that the quenching of the probe might be dominated by static quenching. Static quenching resulted from the formation of a nonfluorescent ground state complex between fluorophore and quencher. Thermodynamic parameters indicated that this combination was a spontaneous exothermic process. The detection limits for chlorpyrifos, endosulfan, heptachlor, and malathion were 0.47, 1.02, 0.66, and 0.64 μM, respectively. This method had been applied to detect the pesticides in tap water, river water and wastewater.

They also synthesized a Eu3+-complex of 1:2 stoichiometric ratio using bathophenanthroline (Scheme S2, compound 9) as a ligand to detect several pesticides [37]. Azinphos ethyl, malathion, and heptachlor could effectively quench the fluorescent emission at 614 nm of the probe with the respective detection limits of 0.68, 0.92, and 0.35 μM in acetonitrile. Similarly, the results of Stern-Volmer studies demonstrated the dominant role of dynamic quenching. Thermodynamic parameters indicated their different responsive mechanisms, the interaction between the probe and azinphos ethyl was electrostatic interaction, the interaction with malathion was mainly due to van der Waals force, and the interaction with heptachlor was resulting from hydrophobic interaction.

In addition, they have studied the interaction between a Eu3+-complex with allyl-3-carboxycoumarin ligand and the pesticides chlorpyrifos, endosulfan, and crotoxyphos [38]. The detection limits for these pesticides were 6.53, 0.004, 3.72 μM, respectively. Chlorpyrifos and crotoxyphos had a significant quenching effect on the fluorescent emission at 614 nm of Eu3+-complex. However, endosulfan had a fluorescent enhancement effect at 614 nm of Eu3+-complex, and the fluorescent had the largest change in ethanol. Thermodynamic parameters indicated that the reaction between Eu3+-complex and chlorpyrifos and crotoxyphos was spontaneous, mainly due to hydrophobic interactions, while the interaction between Eu3+-complex and endosulfan attributed to electrostatic interactions.

In 2016, they have synthesized a strong fluorescent Eu3+-complex using o-(4-methoxy benzoyl) benzoic acid [o-(4-anisoyl)] (Scheme S2, compound 10) as a ligand for the detection of pesticides in ethanol-water (5:5, v/v) solution [39]. Sensitive detection of pesticides was achieved due to the quench effect of azinphos ethyl, chlorfenvinphos, diazinon, and isofenphos to the characteristic emission peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm. The detection limits of azinphos ethyl, chlorfenvinphos, diazinon, and isofenphos were 1.93, 2.82, 3.78, and 1.80 μM, respectively. The quenching mechanism of chlorfenvinphos, diazinon, and isofenphos to the probe was dynamic quenching, while that of azinphos was static quenching.

And in 2017, Azab et al. have synthesized Tb3+-complex terbium-[Ethyl-4-hydroxy-1-(4-methoxyphenyl)-2-quinolinone-3-carboxylate] (Scheme S2, compound 11) fluorescent to detect pesticides crotoxyphos and malathion [40]. The combination of the Tb3+-complex and pesticides caused fluorescent quenching at 545 nm, which was proved to be static quenching by Stern-Volmer. Subsequently, they studied thermodynamics, and the results showed that the interaction between the probe and pesticides (crotoxyphos and malathion) was electrostatic interaction in water, and the interaction between the probe and malathion was van der Waals force and hydrogen bonding in ethanol. The detection limit of the probe was 1.7 μM for crotoxyphos in water, 0.94 μM for malathion in ethanol, and 2.68 μM for malathion in water. However, when these two pesticides were present at the same time, the probe could not be able to distinguish them.

Other researchers had also contributed to pesticides detection by using metal complexes as the fluorescent probe. Lu et al. have synthesized a novel cholesterol derivative, 4-chloro-7-nitro-1,2,3-benzoxadiazole (CTN) (Scheme S2, compound 5), using triazole as a linker [41]. When the concentration of CTN was lower than 1 μM, two fluorescent emission peaks of 425 and 527 nm could be observed. However, only an emission peak of 527 nm was observed when the concentration of CTN was greater than 50 μM, due to the excessive concentration of CTN leading to fluorescent quenching of 425 nm. After the addition of Hg2+, a CTN-Hg
2+ complex was formed, and the fluorescent emission at 527 nm was weakened and moved to the longer wavelengths 561 nm. Subsequently, five organophosphorus pesticides, glyphosate, dichlorvos, chlorpyrifos, diazinon, and phoxim, were used as analytes, with the detection limit of 0.015, 0.018, 0.087, 0.098, and 0.113 μg/mL. With the presence of any of these pesticides, the fluorescent emission gradually increased and the blue shift occurred from 561 nm to 527 nm. Decomposition of the CTN-Hg
2+ complex was caused by the combination of Hg2+ and pesticides.

A Tb3+-complex between Tb3+ and the ligand 3-ally-salicylohydrazide (Scheme S2, compound 6) in ethanol with the 1:2 (Tb3+: ligand) molar ratio has been synthesized by Ibrahim et al. as a fluorescent probe for the detection of pesticide dichlorvos [42]. The probe had a very stable luminescence property in ethanol and was sensitive to dichlorvos. With the addition of dichlorvos, the fluorescent at 546 nm was significantly enhanced. In addition, the interaction between the probe and the dichlorvos was calculated by the Benesi-Hildebrand equation. Thermodynamic studies have shown that the reaction was an endothermic reaction and more advantageous in high temperatures.

Hu et al. have synthesized ligand (Scheme S2, compound 12) by modifying the structure of 4-hydroxybenzlidene imidazolinone with nitrogen-containing heterocyclic 1,10-phenanthroline to form ligand-Eu3+ accompanied by the removal of coordinated water molecules and an intramolecular energy transfer from ligand to Eu3+ [43]. The complex ligand-Eu3+ had fluorescent emission due to energy transfer. With the addition of methyl parathion, it was easier to combine with Eu3+ than ligand, resulting in fluorescent quenching at 617 nm. The detection limit of methyl parathion for this method was 95 nM.

Recently our group designed and synthesized an organic small molecule fluorescent probe which could specifically detect glyphosate using the Cu2+-indicator displacement strategy [44]. Cu2+ could combine with the two pyridines and a nitrogen center structure in the probe through chelation. This process could quench the fluorescent of the probe because the attraction of Cu2+ to the electron pair of rhodamine caused the conversion of rhodamine from helical rings to quinones to be blocked. However, in the presence of glyphosate, due to the stronger chelation between glyphosate and Cu2+, the inhibited intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) in the probe was restored and the fluorescent was enhanced (Fig. 3 ). The method for detecting glyphosate was accompanied by color changes (from colorless to yellow-green under 365 nm UV lamp, and from colorless to pink under natural light) and had the potential to detect glyphosate with the naked eye. The fluorescent response time of this probe to glyphosate was only 2 min, which was the fastest response among glyphosate sensors reported so far. The detection limit of this method for glyphosate was 4.1 nM, and this method could sensitively detect glyphosate on the surface of soil and cabbage.

Calixarene, pillararene and cucurbituril can specifically bind to the probe to form a host-guest complex due to their unique cavity structure, when pesticides are added, the binding ability between pesticides and macrocyclic molecules is stronger, which will destroy the original host-guest complex and form a new host-guest system, resulting in fluorescence enhancement or quenching, so as to realize the detection of different pesticides [45,46]. For example, Zeng et al. synthesized a naphthol-appended calix [4]arene (NOC4) (Scheme S3, compound 14), then clicked it to a microstructured Au surface by click chemistry for the specific detection of metolcarb [47]. NOC4 was hydrophobic because of the tert-butyl group on the surface of NOC4. When metolcarb and NOC4 formed a stable host-guest complex through π-π interaction, the host-guest complex was converted to hydrophilic as a result of metolcarb inducing the hydrogen bonding of the amide group in the water. At the same time, the fluorescent emission at 340 nm of the host-guest complex was significantly enhanced, indicating that it had high selectivity to metolcarb with the detection limit of 0.1 μM.

Yu et al. synthesized a two-dimensional MOF nanosheet decorated with tetra-pyridyl calix [4] arene as a chemosensor to detect glyphosate [48]. The large surface area and multiple highly active sites on the MOF nanosheets increased the sensing speed and sensitivity of glyphosate. A pyridine group in the calix [4]arene ligand did not coordinate with the cadmium atom and could rotate freely through the methylene group, so the size and shape of the cavity could be adjusted freely, which was benefit to combine with glyphosate through the host-guest interaction. The combination of glyphosate and calix [4]arene increased the rigidity of the backbone, promoting the charge transfer to significantly enhance the fluorescent emission at 329 nm. Moreover, the rigidity of MOF-Calix nanosheets increased with temperature decreasing, resulting in an increase in fluorescent intensity. The method could rapidly detect glyphosate within 1 min and the detection limit was as low as 2.25 μM.

There were also turn-off calixarene based hydrocarbon fluorescent sensors for pesticide detection. Zhang et al. reported a coumarin-pillar [5]arene derivative (P
5
C
10 ) (Scheme S3, compound 13) as a fluorescent sensor to detect pesticide [49]. P
5
C
10 could specifically recognize methyl-parathion in the interference of other organic pesticides. The fluorescent of P
5
C
10 at 391 nm could be quenched by methyl-parathion. P
5
C
10 has a high binding affinity to methyl-parathion, and the association constant of P5C10 was 2.38 × 104 M. Electron-deficient methyl-parathion and π-rich cavity of the host pillar [5]arene lead to the formation of a host-guest compound within a 1:1 complex mode by π-π stacking interactions, resulting in fluorescent quenching.

A host-guest complex (Q[8]-ThT) formed by the cucurbit [8]uril (Q[8]) and Thioflavin (ThT) was reported by Song et al. for the detection of herbicides paraquat and diquat in aqueous solutions [50]. As the concentration increased of paraquat and diquat, the fluorescent intensity of the Q[8]-ThT complex at 570 nm gradually decreased companying with a blue shift, while the fluorescent intensity at 490 nm increased. The fluorescent quenching resulted from the formation of ternary complexes through part of the ThT entering the Q[8] cavity and introducing a herbicide at the same time (Fig. 4 ). The detection limit of paraquat and diquat was 7.95 nM and 8.07 nM.

Panchal et al. synthesized bidansylated oxacalix [4]arene (BDO) (Scheme S3, Compound 15) as a turn-off fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of pendimethalin, which can form a low energy inclusion complex with BDO to cause the fluorescent quenching at 666 nm [51]. The detection limit of this method can reach 3.81 nM. The experiments of molecular docking and dynamic simulations demonstrated that low energy inclusion complexes were feasible with an optimum of one intra H-bond and numerous hydrophobic contacts.

In some cases, the electron-rich calixarene cavity was designed to combine with an electron-deficient fluorescent dye, which can lead to the fluorescent quenching of the dye due to the spatial energy transfer. After adding the pesticide, because of thr more strong interaction, the pesticide can combine with the calixarene so that the fluorescent dye was released from the calixarene cavity to recover fluorescent. For example, Mao et al. reported a hydrazino-pillar [5]arene (HP) modified graphene (HP-G) (Scheme S3, compound 16) as a turn-on probe for paraquat detection in living cells and mice [52]. HP-G recognized paraquat by selective host-guest interactions, which converted weak interactions into sensitive fluorescent signals. The electron-rich cavity of HP combined with the electron-deficient dye indicator safranine T, inducing safranine T attaching to graphene by the electrostatic interaction between safranine T and HP. The fluorescent of safranine T was quenched resulting from the fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) between safranine T and graphene. After the addition of paraquat, the stronger electrostatic interaction between paraquat and HP molecules leads to the release of safranine T, thereby further inhibiting the energy transfer between graphene and safranine T.

Tan et al. developed a compound of phosphate pillar [5]arene (PWP5) (Scheme S3, compound 17), and then modified it onto reduced graphene (PWP5-rGO) as a receptor to detect paraquat [53]. The dye indicator Safranine T (ST) was bound to PWP5-rGO, causing the fluorescent quenching of ST. After the competitive paraquat was added, the positively charged paraquat and the negatively charged PWP5-rGO were tightly bound by the electrostatic interaction, causing the release of ST, and the fluorescent recovery was observed. The detection limit of this method was 0.0035 μM. Furthermore, it has been applied to the detection of paraquat in tap water and lakes. The recovery was 97.5–101.7% and the RSDs was 2.0–4.9%.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted significant attention in the fields of adsorption and luminescence sensing due to their rich topology, tunable pore size and shape, high surface area, and abundant active sites. Luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) have sprung up as great potential chemical sensors to detect pesticides with fast response, high sensitivity, high selectivity and easy operation. The fluorescence-based sensing mechanisms mainly include the following: the change in the skeleton of a complex, the weak interaction between the uncoordinated action sites of the ligand and the analytes, π-π interaction, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or a combination of the above, which depend on the structure and electron properties of both MOFs and pesticides [54,55]. Furthermore, most of the MOFs can not only specifically recognize pesticides, but also can remove pesticides by the excellent adsorption capacity to pesticides.

For example, Xu et al. have used 1,2,4,5-Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)benzene (H4TCPB) and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O to synthesize a highly luminescent ZnPO-MOF as a chemosensor to monitor methyl parathion (Fig. 5 a) [56]. The electron-rich nitrobenzene moiety in parathion-methyl could cause PET to quench the fluorescent. After the addition of methyl parathion, the fluorescent emission of ZnPO-MOF rapidly quenched in a few seconds, which demonstrated that the ZnPO-MOF had high sensitivity toward methyl parathion. Using other pesticides and some nitrobenzene compounds as interference, the sensor still showed good selectivity to methyl parathion. The detection limit of this method was as low as 0.456 nM, which has been applied to the detection of parathion-methyl in irrigation water.

Yang et al. prepared a novel Zr-MOF, Fe3O4@SiO2@UiO-67 through a versatile layer by layer assembly strategy for sensitive detection and simultaneous removal of glyphosate [57]. The fluorescent was enhanced after the interaction between Zr-MOF with glyphosate (Fig. 5b). The sensor exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards glyphosate due to the high affinity of Zr–OH for phosphate groups. The detection limit of this method was 0.093 μg/L and Zr-MOF exhibited excellent adsorption performance with a high adsorption capacity (256.54 mg g−1). Zr-MOF could be recycled and reused at least four times, which had great potential in purifying water.

Xu et al. have synthesized a novel amino-decorated bridging ligand H2APDA (Scheme S4, compound 18), which be used to react with Mg2+ to form a new Mg-MOF [58]. There were many electron-donating amino groups in the Mg-MOF, which could recognize electron-deficient 2,6-dichloro-4-nitroaniline (DCN) by electron transfer to induce the fluorescent quenching. The method had good selectivity towards DCN in the presence of other pesticides, and the fluorescent intensity and quenching efficiency of Mg-MOF could retain after five repeated uses. The detection limit for DCN was 150 ppb.

Xu et al. have synthesized a rigid conjugated tricarboxylic acid ligand 4,4'-(9-(4′-carboxy-[1,1′-biphenyl]-4-yl)-9H-carbazole-3,6-diyl)dibenzoic acid (H3CBCD) (Scheme S4, compound 20), introducing the carbazole into the ligand for the first time, and used this ligand to react with Cd2+ to form a Cd-LMOF [59]. The rigid and π-conjugated structure of the ligand resulted in a strong blue fluorescent emission of Cd-CBCD. Electron transfer between the electron-rich Cd-LMOF and the electron-deficient pesticide DCN caused fluorescent quenching of Cd-CBCD. In addition to PET, the resonance energy transfer (RET) process also led to fluorescent quenching due to the overlap between the emission spectrum of Cd-CBCD and the absorption spectrum of DCN. The detection limit of this method for DCN was 145 ppb.

A yttrium-based MOF with europium and terbium co-doping [Y1.8Eu0.1Tb0.1(PDA)3(H2O)1]·2H2O (PDA = 1,4-phenylenediacetate) was synthesized by Singha et al. to detect methyl azinphos [60]. Methyl azinphos could specifically quench the fluorescent of the MOF compared to several other pesticides. The π-π interaction between methyl azinphos and the aromatic moiety of the MOF resulted in static quenching. In addition, RET between the absorption spectrum of azinphos-methyl at 290 nm and the emission spectrum of the ligand center of the MOF caused fluorescent quenching. The detection limit for this method reached 0.67 μM (212 ppb).

Tao et al. have reported a luminescent MOF [Zn2(bpdc)2(BPyTPE)] [BPyTPE=(E)-1,2- Diphenyl-1,2-bis(4-(pyridin-4-yl)phenyl)ethene] which was constructed using the TPE-based ligand (BPyTPE) (Scheme S4, compound 19) with remarkable aggregation induced emission (AIE) properties as a pillar linker to detect DCN [61].

AIE was a new type of photoluminescence phenomenon which was named for the first time by Luo et al. [62]. A chemical structure with AIE could produce strong fluorescent in high concentration or solid film state but have no or very weak luminescence in dilute solution state. The intramolecular motion was greatly restricted due to the coordination of BPyTPE in a rigid framework, leading to excellent AIE properties. The MOF could recognize pesticide DCN sensitively because of the PET. On the other hand, hydrogen bonding between the MOF and DCN could increase the sensitivity of detection. The detection limit of this method was 0.13 μg/L, which had been applied to the detection of pesticide residues in vegetables and fruits.

A water-stable luminescent zirconium MOF (Zr-MOF) was synthesized by He et al. to detect methyl parathion in spiked tap water, rainwater, river water, and cowpea samples [63]. PET between electron-deficient methyl parathion and electron-rich MOFs caused fluorescent quenching. The detection limit of this method was as low as 0.438 nM, and rapid detection could be completed within 2.5 min.

Though most of MOFs exhibited a PET mechanism for the pesticide response, there are also some other mechanisms that cause pesticides to quench the fluorescent of MOF. For example, Xu et al. have developed a glove-based wearable sensor for detecting chlorpyrifos [64]. This new chemical sensing glove had good resistance to mechanical deformation in practical applications. The sensor was based on flexible carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) aerogel with two fluorescent centers [red-emitting Eu-MOFs and blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs)]. The interaction between chlorpyrifos and the organic ligand BTB (BTB = 1,3,5-benzenetribenzoic acid) of Eu-MOFs caused the chlorpyrifos to filter the light adsorb by the BTB molecule, reducing the probability of energy transfer from BTB to Eu3+, which suppressed the fluorescent of Eu3+ (Fig. 5c). The mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by chlorpyrifos may be based on the interaction between the organic ligands in Eu-MOFs and chlorpyrifos, which can disturb the process of ligand absorption or energy migration. At the same time, the sensor also had a sensitive colorimetric reaction. As the fluorescent of Eu-MOFs was quenched, the color changed from bright red to purple and finally to dark blue. The detection limit of this method was 50 nM, which had been applied to the detection of chlorpyrifos on the surface of vegetables and fruits.

Guo et al. have prepared a 2D Zn-based MOF (Zn-MOF-1) formulated as [Zn2(L)2(TPA)]·2H2O [L = 4-(tetrazol-5-yl) Phenyl-4, 20:60, 400-terpyridine, TPA = terephthalic acid] with blue fluorescent to detect pesticides DCN [65]. Since the excitation light of Zn-MOF-1 was absorbed by DCN and the electronic interaction between DCN and organic ligands (L and TPA) in the Zn-MOF-1, the fluorescent of Zn-MOF-1 was quenched. The fluorescent intensity was essentially unchanged in the five repeated uses of the Zn-MOF-1. The detection limit for this method was 1.90 μM (0.39 μg/L).

One of the advantages of nanomaterials as fluorescent sensors was easy to prepare [66,67], such as Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) and Qiu et al. prepared AuNPs using the reduction method of sodium citrate [68]. Nanomaterials can be combined with a fluorogen (fluorescent molecule or QDs) through inner-filter effect (IFE) [69,70] or FRET [71,72], causing the fluorescent changing of the fluorogen. After adding the pesticide, the pesticide can be combined with the nanomaterial, leading to releasing the fluorogen and restoring the fluorescent. Fluorescent turn-on sensors also increase detection sensitivity.

Zhan et al. have designed an intrinsic dual-emitting Mn-doped ZnS nanocrystal (NC) with emission peaks at 435 nm and 600 nm, respectively [73]. The NC exhibited the change of fluorescent color between blue and red which can rely on the naked eye to detect pesticide diethylphosphorothioate (DEP). The probe was based on dopamine dithiocarbamate (DDTC: carbon disulfide formed a dithiocarbamate with the amine group of dopamine) which could cause fluorescent quenching at 600 nm of the NC due to PET on the NC surface while fluorescent at 435 nm unchanged as a reference moiety. As adding the DEP, the fluorescent at 600 nm gradually enhanced. Due to the strong coordination ability of DEP with many transition metal ions, the color of the solution changed from dark blue to purple to bright red (Fig. 6 ). In this method, the probe's transmitted signal had a reference emission, which eliminated background interference, greatly improved the accuracy. The limit of detection was 1.8 μM, and the method had been adopted for more convenient tape test strips.

Based on the IFE effect, Wei and his colleagues also made a similar report [74]. They have prepared nano-cerium dioxide as a recognition component, which hydrolyzed organophosphorus compounds and pesticides to form p-nitrophenol and produce fluorescent. However, CQDs caused the fluorescent quenching by the IFE effect (Fig. 7 ). Unlike traditional enzyme-based detection methods, this study proposed a novel enzyme-free detection method. The limit of detection of this method was 0.375 μM and was applied to detect methy-paraoxonl in spiked Panax quinquefolius and tap water samples.

Hung et al. have established the rhodamine B-AuNPs system [75]. The combination of rhodamine B and AuNPs can cause fluorescent quenching based on FRET effect. In the presence of dimethoate, dimethoate replaced Rhodamine B on the surface of AuNPs, and the fluorescent of Rhodamine B was recovered (Fig. 8 a). The method had a limit of detection of 0.004 μg/L and was used for the detection of dimethoate in water samples and fruits.

Similarly, Luo et al. have proposed a Rhodamine B (RB) modified bimetallic nanoparticle system (RB-Ag/AuNPs), and the fluorescent of RB-Ag/AuNPs was quenched before the addition of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) (Fig. 8b) [76]. Because the coordination ability of Ag/AuNPs and OPs was stronger than that of Ag/AuNPs and RB, RB would be replaced from the surface of Ag/AuNPs in the presence of OPs, accompanied by fluorescent recovery of RB. The system combined organic and inorganic nanocomposite fluorescent sensors for detecting OPs in actual fruit and water samples with a detection limit as low as 0.0018 ng/mL.

An off-on fluorescent sensor was constructed by Wang et al. to detect malathion [77]. The combination of AgNPs-β-cyclodextrins hybrid material and the probe 2,3-dihydro-5-oxo-5H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) based on the FRET could cause the fluorescent quenching of TPCA at 420 nm. After adding malathion, malathion bound to AgNPs-β-cyclodextrins, resulting in TPCA releasing and fluorescent recovery (Fig. 9 a). The limit of detection for malathion was 0.1 μg/mL.

There are also fluorescent sensors based on the combination of quantum dots and nanomaterials to detect pesticides. Quantum dots with good luminescence properties can be used as fluorescent signal elements [78,79]. The interaction between fluorescent quantum dots and nanomaterials will cause fluorescent quenching due to FRET or PET. And the sensors formed with quantum dots and nanomaterials can provide higher sensitivity and better selectivity for the detection of pesticides.

For example, Yang and his coworkers have established N, P-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs), and AuNPs system based on the FRET effect study to detect carbendazim [80]. In the system, AuNPs significantly inhibited the green fluorescent of N and P-CQDs. When carbendazim was added, the specific interaction between carbendazim and AuNPs led to the fluorescent recovery of N, P-CQDs (Fig. 9b). The detection limit of carbendazim was 0.002 μM.

An AuNPs-QDs system has been created by Xu et al., in which ratiometric fluorescent quantum dots (RF-QDs) were combined with AuNPs to cause fluorescent quenching of RF-QDs due to the IFE effect [81]. In the presence of the pesticide acetamiprid, the cyano group of acetamiprid had a good affinity with AuNPs and could be adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs, and the color changed from purple to dark blue during aggregation (Fig. 10 ). Therefore, AuNPs also have an effect of colorimetric detection. At the same time, the IFE effect of AuNPs on RF-QDs was weakened, and the fluorescent intensity of RF-QDs was restored. The limit of detection was 16.8 μg/L.

Wang et al. have designed a fluorescent-visualized paper-based off-on sensor with double quantum dots and nanoporphyrin to detect pesticides [82]. When nanoporphyrin was added to the double quantum dots (CdTe and ZnCdSe), the fluorescent of the double quantum dots was quenched. The quenching of CdTe was due to PET, and the quenching of ZnCdSe was due to PET and FRET. As the pesticides were added continuously, the electron-rich pesticide and the dodecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (DTAB) strong covered on the surface of the nanoporphyrin were combined by strong electrostatic action, which destroyed the weak electrostatic interaction between the double quantum dot and the nanoporphyrin, resulting in the fluorescent of the double quantum dot restoring (Fig. S1). This method used double quantum dots as the signal amplification part compared to the single quantum dot quenching effect, which improved the quenching efficiency and the detection sensitivity of pesticides. It had been found through this research that the quenching phenomenon only occurred between double quantum dots and nanoporphyrins, which effectively reduced background interference and improved sensitivity. Because of the different binding abilities of nanoporphyrin with the three pesticides, dimethoate, dichlorvos, and demeton, it was possible to accurately distinguish them. However, although the fluorescent recovery of CdTe was achieved in the paper-based sensor, the color change was not obvious, and the color of ZnCdSe on the paper was weak so that the fluorescent quenching and recovery effects were not obvious. This method was only suitable for semi-quantitative and qualitative analysis.

Quantum dot, as a new type of nanomaterial, has an excellent performance in luminescence [83–85]. QDs were semiconductor nanocrystals with a diameter of less than 10 nm and they had unique properties such as size and shape-dependent optical properties and discrete electronic density of states which was the result of the quantum confinement effect [86]. It can combine with some small molecules [87] and metal ions [88] to cause its fluorescent change. In the presence of the target pesticide, the fluorescent can be restored due to the combination between pesticide and QDs or metal ions. Some semi-doped or surface-functionalized QDs also exhibit excellent luminescence and sensing performance. Pesticide fluorescent sensors using quantum dots as identification elements have usually high sensitivity and good selectivity.

For example, Zhang et al. have introduced dithizone on the surface of CdTe QDs for sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos [89]. In alkaline media, the fluorescent of CdTe QDs was quenched due to the FRET process of dithizone on the surface of CdTe QDs (Fig. S2). With the addition of chlorpyrifos, dithizone was replaced by the hydrolyzate of chlorpyrifos. There was a PS bond in the hydrolyzate of chlorpyrifos, which could be more stably bound to the surface of CdTe QDs, accompanied by fluorescent recovery of CdTe QDs. This method had a detection limit of chlorpyrifos as low as 0.1 nM.

A similar work has been reported that the dithizone (DZ)-CdTe QDs fluorescent quenching system was designed based FRET by Sheng et al. [90]. After the addition of dimethoate (DMT), dithizone was replaced by dimethoate and dithizone was separated from the surface of the QDs, resulting in the fluorescent recovery of CdTe QDs (Fig. S3). The detection limit of this method was 0.005 g/mL.

Fan et al. have reported a turn-off fluorescent data array sensor for identification and detection of pesticides paraquat, chlorpyrifos, dipterex, methyl thiophanate and cartap [91]. In this work, the approach of two water soluble ZnCdSe and CdSe QDs simultaneously to detect analyte was established. These five pesticides had different degrees of fluorescent quenching on the two ZnCdSe and CdSe QDs, resulting in changes in the intensity and position of the two fluorescent emission peaks. Experiments showed that the fluorescent quenching ability of chlorpyrifos was the best while the methyl thiophanate was the worst. The detection limit of the pesticides for the method was 0.2 nM, which has been used for the detection of various real samples such as tea, Chinese herbal water extract, and wastewater.

Zhang et al. have designed a system formed with Mn-doped ZnS QD and Ag+ as a fluorescent turn-on sensor for the detection of pesticide thiram [92]. The fluorescent of ZnS:Mn2+ QDs was quenched by silver ions on the surface of the QDs through the cation exchange reaction based on the higher Ksp of AgS than ZnS. After the addition of thiram, a stable thiram-Ag+ complex formed, resulting in the fluorescent recovery of QDs (Fig. S4a). The fluorescent phenomenon of “turn-on” made the detection more visualized and sensitive. The detection limit of this method was 25 nM, which has been applied to the detection of thiram in fresh peels.

Sahoo et al. have used zinc oxide quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor to detect and distinguish four pesticides, aldrin, tetradifon, glyphosate, and atrazine [93]. Aldrin and tetradifon were rich in leaving group Cl, which could be replaced by primary amine groups on the surface of zinc oxide through nucleophilic substitution reaction, resulting in fluorescent quenching (Fig. 9). Although atrazine also had a chlorine atom, however, due to the steric hindrance of the presence of aminoalkyl groups, the nucleophilic substitution reaction of the chlorine atom was restricted. Furthermore, the chlorine atom of atrazine is attached to a heteroaromatic ring and does not undergo standard nucleophilic substitution reactions in any event. So the binding capacity of atrazine with quantum dots was the lowest among the four pesticides. At last, the carboxylic acid group of glyphosate and the primary amine group of quantum dots would be bonded by electrostatic adsorption and hydrogen bonding (Fig. S5). The difference between the binding capacity of these four pesticides and quantum dots could be used to distinguish them.

Quantum dots, was also known as semiconductor nanocrystals (NCs). carbon quantum dots was a kind of quantum dots, which was prepared with carbon element. Carbon quantum dots are a class of fluorescent nanoparticles that include graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [94], polymer dots [95], and carbon nanodots [96] have found several applications in fluorescence sensing [97,98]. These types of QDs offer unique fluorescent properties and have the advantage of low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [99,100] compared to other QDs.

Mohapatra et al. have synthesized nitrogen doped carbon quantum dot by hydrothermal method with orthophenylene diamine as a turn-on fluorescent sensor for the detection of pesticide atrazine based on AIE [101]. The amino group on the surface of the carbon quantum dot could form a hydrogen bond with the atrazine at lower pH, to form a rigid planar structure that restricts intramolecular motion, resulting in enhanced fluorescent (Fig. S6). The detection limit for this method was 3 μg/L of atrazine, and this sensor has been applied in real agricultural samples and bacterial cells.

Carbon dots codoped with magnesium and nitrogen (Mg, N-CDs) have been synthesized by Peng et al. Then they used pralidoxime (PAM) to modify Mg, N-CDs to establish a Mg@ N-CD-PAM system for the detection of pesticide paraoxon [102]. Mg and N doping in CDs improved the fluorescent properties of CDs due to Mg and N doping altering the electronic and optical characteristics of CDs and introducing many active sites on CDs surface. In the Mg, N-CD-PAM system, the addition of paraoxon could lead to fluorescent quenched. Because PAM shortened the distance of Mg, N-CDs, and paraoxon, facilitating the electron transfer process (Fig. S4b). The detection limit of this method was 0.87 nM.

Du et al. have used the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to create an N-GQDs/Hg
2+ system as a fluorescent turn-on sensor for the detection of paraquat [103]. In the N-GQDs/Hg
2+ system, Hg2+ could completely quench the fluorescent of N-GQDs. After the addition of paraquat, paraquat could bind to Hg2+, resulting in the release of N-GQDs and restoring the fluorescent (Fig. S7). The detection limit of this method was 19 μg/L.

In this review, we discussed about developments and applications of non-biological fluorescent sensors for determining pesticides in the last 8 years. According to different recognition materials, non-biological fluorescent sensors can be divided into organic functional small molecules, calixarenes and pillararenes, metal organic framework systems, and nanomaterials. We have summarized the design method of these kinds of non-biological fluorescent sensors, the corresponding mechanism to detect different pesticides, the detection limit of each pesticide, and their application in actual samples. Meanwhile, we have also briefly introduced the advantages of each type fluorescent chemosensors. Small molecule and calixarene based hydrocarbon chemosensors have superiority in selective detection because of their specific binding to pesticides. In addition to detecting pesticides, metal organic framework based chemosensors can also absorb pesticides to reduce pollution. Nanomaterials based chemosensors can be easily prepared. However, these sensors also have some disadvantages. In general, small molecule and calixarene based hydrocarbon chemosensors are more difficult to prepare and take longer time than metal organic framework and nanomaterials based chemosensors. However, metal organic framework and nanomaterials based chemosensors had poorer specific recognition of pesticides than small molecule and calixarene based hydrocarbon chemosensors. Therefore, probes with good selectivity and sensitivity to pesticides that are easy to prepare and synthesize are expected to be developed and obtained in the future.
